coredata.dc:title: Focusing on the bottom contact: Carbon quantum dots embedded SnO2 electron transport layer for high-performance and stable perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jing
Yin, Yanfeng
He, Bingchen
Wang, Pengfei
Wang, Minhuan
Cai, Wanxian
Han, Yaling
Su, Zhenhuang
Guo, Jingya
Cai, Rui
Jin, Shengye
Gao, Xingyu
Bian, Jiming
Shi, Yantao
coredata.dc:description: Rapid progress has been made to achieve high power conversion efficiency (PCE) of metal halide perovskite solar cells in recent years. However, the unsatisfying operational stability still acts as the key factor that obstructs their successful commercialization. Although numerous strategies including grain boundary passivation, surface post-treatment, and processing management have been widely applied to enhance the stability of the perovskite solar cells with n-i-p structure, the significance of the bottom contact between electron transport layer (ETL) and perovskite is usually overlooked. Herein, we report a SnO2 ETL embedded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that largely improves the bottom interface between the perovskite layer and ETL. The multiple positive effect of CQDs in interfacial modulation were confirm by a variety of sophisticated characterizations. The PCE increased from 21.62% to 24.05% owing to the facilitated charge extraction. Notably, the perovskite solar cells with the CQD-SnO2 ETL retained over 84% of their initial PCE after 1000-h continuous irradiation under 1-sun illumination by suppressing the perovskite degradation starting from the bottom contact. This work provides a facile and efficient interface modification method for improving the performance and operational stability of FAPbI3-based PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cells
Carbon quantum dots
Electron transport layer
Interfacial coupling

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). (a) The schematic diagram of the CQDs synthesis process. (b) The UV absorption of the CQDs aqueous dispersion, the insets are photographs of CQD aqueous dispersion. (c) High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) image of the CQDs. (d) and (e) The fast Flourier transform (FFT) and inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) patterns of CQDs, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) of CQDs, SnO2 and CQD-SnO2 powders in 3100–3700 cm−1 (f) and in 500–2000 cm−1 (g).

The surface morphology and electrical properties of SnO2 and CQD-SnO2. (a) and (b) are the top-view scanning electron microscope (SEM) images on FTO substrate. (c) and (d) are atomic force microscopy (AFM) images. (e) The I–V curves are based on the structure of ITO/ETL/Al (shown in the inset). (f) J 1/2-V curves based on the structure of ITO/Al/ETL/Al (shown in the inset). (g) Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectra. (h) The schematic illustration of energy level alignment of each layer in PSCs.

Characterization of the bottom interface of exfoliated perovskite grown on control and CQD-SnO2 ETL. The in-situ XRD patterns during the annealing process for control (a) and CQD-SnO2 ETL (b). (c) Schematic diagram of peeled off perovskite film. The top-view SEM images of the exfoliated perovskite on control (d) and CQD-SnO2 ETL (e). (f) and (g) are the photoluminescence (PL) mapping images. (h) and (i) are time-resolved PL (TRPL) mapping images.

Characterization of the bottom interface of exfoliated perovskite grown on control and CQD-SnO2 ETL. PL intensity of perovskite films based on control (a) and CQD-SnO2 (b) ETL at different delay times after excitation. Diffusion coefficient estimated from time-dependent diffusion profiles. Cross sections of the PL intensity image of perovskite films along the x axis fitted with Gaussian functions at different delay time for perovskite grown on the control (c) and CQD-SnO2 (d) ETL samples, respectively, with the maximum PL signal normalized. (e) σ2 x(t) as a function of delay time, with a linear fit to yield the diffusion coefficient. Top-view scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of perovskite films on SnO2 (f) and CQD-SnO2 (g). The inset is grain size distribution of the perovskite films. TRPL mapping of perovskite films on SnO2 (h) and CQD-SnO2 (i).

Schematic diagram of PSCs structure and characterization of its photovoltaic properties. (a) Schematic of the crystalline mechanism of a perovskite film grown on SnO2 ETL modified by CQDs. (b) Current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the PSCs based on control and CQD-SnO2 ETL. (c) Long-term operational stability obtained by tracking the evolution of normalized PCE from control and CQD-SnO2 devices, under maximum power point (MPP) tracking and continuous light irradiation in N2 glovebox. (d) Long-term thermal stability of the devices stored at 85 °C drying oven in air (∼0% RH). (e) and (f) are the cross-section SEM images of PSCs after operational stability measurement. (g) and (h) are the cross-section SEM images of PSCs after thermal stability measurement.

head.ce:title: Focusing on the bottom contact: Carbon quantum dots embedded SnO2 electron transport layer for high-performance and stable perovskite solar cells

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid progress has been made to achieve high power conversion efficiency (PCE) of metal halide perovskite solar cells in recent years. However, the unsatisfying operational stability still acts as the key factor that obstructs their successful commercialization. Although numerous strategies including grain boundary passivation, surface post-treatment, and processing management have been widely applied to enhance the stability of the perovskite solar cells with n-i-p structure, the significance of the bottom contact between electron transport layer (ETL) and perovskite is usually overlooked. Herein, we report a SnO2 ETL embedded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that largely improves the bottom interface between the perovskite layer and ETL. The multiple positive effect of CQDs in interfacial modulation were confirm by a variety of sophisticated characterizations. The PCE increased from 21.62% to 24.05% owing to the facilitated charge extraction. Notably, the perovskite solar cells with the CQD-SnO2 ETL retained over 84% of their initial PCE after 1000-h continuous irradiation under 1-sun illumination by suppressing the perovskite degradation starting from the bottom contact. This work provides a facile and efficient interface modification method for improving the performance and operational stability of FAPbI3-based PSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Y. Shi and J. Bian: Supervision, Conceived the work; M. Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition; J. Liu and P. Wang fabricated and characterized PSCs; P. Wang and Y. Han: characterized PSCs; R. Cai, S. Jin, Z. Su and X. Gao provided software and equipment help; W. Cai and J. Guo: Visualization, Investigation; J. Liu, Y. Yin and B. He: Writing - Reviewing, Editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, and Data curation.

Since 2012, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) as one of the frontiers in photovoltaics have drawn enormous attention from both academic and industrial communities because of their ever-increasing power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) enabled by the inherently excellent photoelectric properties of metallic halide perovskites, including high light extinction coefficient (∼105 cm−1), adjustable band gap (1.0 eV–3.1 eV), long carrier diffusion length, robust defect tolerance, and facile solution processibility [1–4]. Over the past few years, many efforts have been made to explore some effective strategies to tackle the defect-assisted charge recombination, mismatched energy level alignment, and retarded charge transfer kinetics in PSCs, by which a high certified PCE of 25.7% has been reached, comparable to that of the state-of-the-art single crystalline silicon photovoltaics [5–7]. It is believed that there is still room for PCE improvement if the energy loss occurring in PSCs can be further minimized. For example, the interfacial defects, whose density is 1–2 orders of magnitude higher than that within perovskite film, are an important source of energy loss and should be eliminated or passivated deliberately [8,9]. Meanwhile, the defects in perovskite or at corresponding interfaces are also detrimental to the long-term operational stability of PSCs since they can offer convenient pathways for ion migration and moisture or oxygen penetration [10].

In recent years, defect passivation or interfacial tuning has been intensively reported to improve the PCE and long-term durability of PSCs [11–17]. So far, many strategies have been developed to passivate the defects at the top surface of perovskite or tune the energy level alignment between perovskite and hole transport layer (HTL) using organic molecules, inorganic modifiers, polymers and so on [18,19]. As an example, Gao and co-workers reported two structurally alike polyaromatic molecules with different molecular dipoles to facilitate band energy alignment and passivate defect density of perovskite film [20]. In addition, White et al. introduced a 2D perovskite layer based on octyl ammonium chloride to passivate the defects at the 3D perovskite top surface or in the bulk to achieve a high open-circuit voltage (V oc) of 1.24 V [16]. Whereas, compared to the top surface passivation, it is relatively difficult to conduct bottom surface modification of perovskite film (as in the case of a n-i-p cell architecture) or tune the interfacial characteristics between perovskite and electron transport layer (ETL), where energy loss caused by defects, mismatched energy level alignment or sluggish electron transfer kinetics is also serious. To address this issue, Yang's team introduced Na2WO4 to tin oxide (SnO2) as ETL to reduce defects and enhance the electron extraction capability [21]. Zhang et al. added ammonium salt in SnO2 to carry out pre-crystallization of perovskite, thereby improving the interface electrical contact and device performance [22]. Here, another issue to be noted is that, unlike the top surface, the characterizations of the bottom surface of perovskite are also difficult due to its non-exposed and inaccessible feature [23]. Recently, Zhu and co-workers put forward a simple and effective method to lift off perovskite film from ETL, enabling the bottom side of perovskite and the contact interface to be exposed for facile characterization [24]. At current stage, it is still highly desirable to develop effective modifiers strategies to tune the interface of perovskite/ETL to facilitate charge transfer kinetics and reduce energy loss.

Carbon quantum dot (CQD) is a kind of zero-dimensional carbon material and has many advantages, such as excellent optical properties, good water solubility, electrical conductivity, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and wide raw material source [25]. In recent years, CQDs have been reported to be good candidates as additives or modifiers to promote the electrical properties of ETL or HTL, or tune the crystallization of perovskite. For example, Choy and co-workers reported an imidazole bromide functionalized graphene quantum dot to regulate the interface between the ETL and perovskite layer to achieve high efficiency [26]. Besides, Chen et al. reported the use of l-Cystine and O-phenylenediamine derived CQDs to dope SnO2 to increase its electron mobility [27]. It is believed that there is still a large room to use CQD to improve the performance of PSCs due to their versatility enabled by size effect and diverse and abundant surface functional groups.

In this work, red-emitting CQDs (0.5–3.0 nm) with abundant surface carboxyl and amino groups were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) and neutral red (NR) as precursors. Then, the as-synthesized CQDs as modifiers were introduced to low-temperature solution-processed SnO2 ETL to improve its electrical characters and meanwhile to tune the interfacial properties between ETL and FAPbI3 perovskite. It was revealed that the CQDs-SnO2 had improved the morphology of SnO2 ETL, enhanced electron mobility, and reduced interface defects. In the meantime, the functional groups on the surface of CQDs could guide perovskite crystallization into large grains. Based on the CQDs-SnO2 interface modification strategy, the PCE increased significantly, and much improved operational stability was also achieved.

Briefly, the CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using CA and NR as precursors, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 a. This kind of CQD has been used as a fluorescent probe for peculiar detection benefiting from the high quantum yield and fantastic stability against photobleaching [28]. The CQDs were chosen to be applied in PSCs owing to their versatile structural features, including size effect, adjustable semiconductor characteristics, as well as abundant surface functional groups like carboxyl and amino groups. Firstly, the optical and structural properties of the synthesized CQDs were characterized and shown in Fig. 1b–e. It can be found in Fig. 1b that the aqueous dispersion of CQDs exhibits multiple characteristic absorptions, as indicated by the arrows at about 300 nm, 400 nm and 540 nm, respectively. The one at about 540 nm is from the conjugated structure of NR, suggesting the molecular structure of NR can be well retained after the formation of CQDs. By using a laser scattering particle analyzer, the sizes of CQDs were determined to be in the range of 0.5 nm–3.0 nm (Fig. S1), as subsequently verified by transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image in Fig. 1c. Judged from the crystal lattice in Fig. 1e and corresponding fast flourier transform image (FFT, Fig. 1d), it is clear that the CQD is crystalline rather than amorphous. The lattice spacing is then determined to be about 2.1 Å through inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT, Fig. 1e). Moreover, we used the Flourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) to analyze the functional groups on CQDs, SnO2 and CQDs added into SnO2 (named as CQD-SnO2), as shown in Fig. 1f and g. The bond of C=N, O–H, C=O, C=C, and N–H, which from the original precursors (CA and NR) were seen in the FTIR curve (green line). The Sn–O bond (948 cm−1, existed in SnO2 sample) and –OH (1399 cm−1, existed in CQDs sample) were observed in CQD-SnO2 sample (red line). In addition, a new N–H bond appears at 1565 cm−1, indicating that a new reaction occurs between CQDs and SnO2, which will lead to the change of the properties of SnO2, inevitably.

Since the chemical structure of the two precursors have been entirely retained, there ought to be abundant surface functional groups on CQDs, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino, which can well interact with SnO2 like the new N–H bond. On the other hand, the smaller-sized CQDs may be able to fill the voids formed by the packing of SnO2 nanocrystalline in ETL. Furthermore, it is expected that the CQDs buried can modify the electrical properties of SnO2 ETL since they can play a linker role to promote the electrical connections of SnO2 nanocrystalline. Moreover, the functional groups on the surface of ETL are also expected to be useful for tuning the crystallization of perovskite film subsequently deposited on ETL.

The synthesized CQDs as additives were introduced to SnO2 sol precursor to fabricate ETL through spin coating and systematically characterized. The SnO2 ETL with CQDs was termed as CQD-SnO2, and compared with pure SnO2 ETL (control sample). Here, the top-view scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the two ETL were shown in Fig. 2 a and b. Clearly, the CQD-SnO2 has more uniform surface compared with the pure SnO2. Moreover, atomic force microscopy (AFM) was applied to examine the surface evenness of the control (root mean square (RMS) = 30 nm) and CQD-SnO2 (RMS = 17 nm) ETLs. Compared with the control ETL (Fig. 2c), the CQD-SnO2 ETL has flatter surface morphology (Fig. 2d), indicating that the addition of CQDs facilitates the dispersion of SnO2 nanocrystalline. Subsequently, the trap density (
N t
) and electron mobility (μ e) of control and CQD-SnO2 ETLs were calculated using the space-charge-limited-current (SCLC) method, as shown in Fig. 2e and f, and the corresponding test structure is shown in the insets. In this part, ITO was used as substrate due to its smaller roughness that can guarantee the accuracy of this measurement. The thickness of ETL can be obtained from the cross-section SEM of ETL with perovskite, as shown in Fig. S2. The average thickness of pure SnO2 film is 65 nm and the average thickness of CQD-SnO2 film is 85 nm. The calculated (see calculation detail in the Supplementary Note 1)
N t
are 3.61 × 1016 cm−3 and 1.45 × 1016 cm−3 for control and CQD-SnO2 ETLs, respectively, and the μ e is enhanced from 8.19 × 10−3 cm2V−1s−1 to 2.49 × 10−2 cm2V−1s−1 (see calculation details in Supplementary Note 2). Fewer defects and higher electron mobility are conductive to charge transfer and collection, and a smoother ETL facilitates the subsequent homogeneous nucleation and growth of perovskite film. As aforementioned, the remaining carboxyl and amino groups on the ETL surface might have interacted with the perovskite precursor and then guide perovskite crystallization into large grains [29]. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was used to investigate energy band structure of CQD-SnO2. The UPS spectra of the valence band edge (E onset) and the secondary electron cutoff edge (E cutoff) are depicted in Fig. 2g. The valence band maximum (VBM) can be calculated according to E VB = hν – E cutoff + E onset. The VBM of control and CQD-SnO2 ETLs are 8.02 eV and 7.9 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3a, the optical bandgap (E g) can be deduced from the absorption edge according to E g = 1240/λ, where λ is the absorption edge. The bandgap of control and CQD-SnO2 film was 3.78 eV. So, the conduction band minimum (CBM) values are inferred to be 4.24 eV and 4.12 eV for control and CQD-SnO2 layer, respectively. In addition, the VBM and bandgap of perovskite film are 5.50 eV and 1.56 eV, respectively (Figs. S3b and c). The energy level diagram is demonstrated according to above results, as shown in Fig. 2h. The interfacial charge transfer kinetics will be promoted and the energy loss will be reduced inevitably due to the improved energy band alignment at the ETL/perovskite interface. The smoother ETL is beneficial to perovskite film growth and carrier transport.

In addition, the in-situ XRD patterns during the annealing process was detected to explore the evolution of the perovskite crystal structure with the annealing time, as shown in Fig. 3 a and b. It can be seen that the control sample required 45 s to be fully converted to perovskite, while the CQD-SnO2 sample could be fully converted to perovskite in 20 s, which indicates that the perovskite films of CQD-SnO2 ETL crystallize faster. The interfacial properties of ETL/perovskite and the bottom perovskite were characterized and analyzed due to it is crucial to the final performance of PSCs. As shown in Fig. 3b, the perovskite film was peeled off from ETL using mechanical separation method (see details in experimental section) [30], by which a large area of intact film can be easily obtained to probe into the bottom of perovskite film and the ETL/perovskite interface, as shown in Fig. S4. The top-view SEM images of bottom surface of perovskite are shown in Fig. 3d and e. The perovskite film deposited on pure SnO2 ETL has a rougher bottom surface with chaotic arrangement and many voids, where defects concentrate and result in serious nonradiative recombination, and the interfacial charge transfer can also be adversely affected. By comparison, the perovskite film deposited on CQD-SnO2 exhibits larger grains closely packed together into a flat surface with less voids.

Subsequently, we characterized the bottom surface of perovskite films through steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved PL (TRPL), as shown in Fig. S5. By comparison, the PL intensity of the perovskite film grown on the surface of CQD-SnO2 is stronger. And the averaged lifetime (τ ave) of 71.3 ns for the perovskite film grown on CQD-SnO2 is remarkably shorter than that (202.4 ns) for the control one, indicating a faster charge transfer kinetics enabled by a strong interfacial coupling between perovskite and CQD-SnO2 ETL as aforementioned, as shown in Table S1. It is consistent with the two-dimensional (2D) PL mapping images with a sub-micrometer spatial resolution, as shown in Fig. 3f–i.

Carrier diffusivity, a key factor for the performance of PSCs, was characterized by PL scanned imaging microscopy to investigate the effect of CQDs-SnO2 ETL on the carrier transport of bottom perovskite films (see the detail about the measurement in the Supplementary Note 3). The PL intensity images at various delay time for control and CQD-SnO2 perovskite films were shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The spread PL shows the carriers transported away from excitation site since the PL intensity is proportional to the number of carriers [31–33]. Here, we used the cross-sectional profiles (Fig. 4c and d) of PL spots at different delay times to determine the diffusion coefficient (D) (see the detail about the calculation in the Supplementary Note 4). The perovskite films based on SnO2 and CQD-SnO2 have D values of 0.0334 ± 0.0082 and 0.0679 ± 0.0117 cm2/s, respectively (Fig. 4e). We can further obtain the carrier diffusion length (LD) (0.49 μm and 1.17 μm for SnO2 and CQD-SnO2, respectively) by
L D
=
D τ
. Obviously, characterizations of the perovskite bottom surface suggest that the CQDs buried in CQD-SnO2 not only can strengthen the interfacial coupling between ETL and perovskite but also enable the formation of large grains through guiding the crystallization of perovskite. These structural advantages are favorable to minimize energy loss caused by nonradiative recombination and accelerate charge transfer across the ETL/perovskite interface.

The 2D GIWAXS patterns of the top surface of perovskite films based on SnO2 and CQD-SnO2 were shown in Fig. S6. The CQD-SnO2 ETL helps to improve the crystallinity of perovskite films obviously. The top surface SEM images can also validate the improved crystallinity of perovskite film deposited on CQD-SnO2 ETL, compared with the one deposited on SnO2 ETL (Fig. 4f and g). In addition, the RMS was reduced to 31 nm from 39 nm when introducing the CQDs into SnO2 as ETL, seen in Fig. S7. Furthermore, the TRPL mapping images of the top surface of perovskite films were measured and shown in Fig. 4h and i, from which we can comprehensively observe a sub-micrometer spatial resolution of carrier lifetime. Clearly, the photogenerated carriers can be extracted rapidly by the CQD-SnO2 ETL.

It can be concluded from above results that the use of CQDs buried in SnO2 ETL, as schematically depicted in Fig. 5 a, can offer integrated benefits for improving ETL's electrical properties, tuning perovskite crystallization, strengthening the interfacial connection between ETL and perovskite, by which the photovoltaic performance of PSCs is expected to be enhanced. For example, the CQDs can assist the dispersion of SnO2 nanocrystalline to obtain ETL film with reduced surface roughness, and can also enhance the conductivity of ETL. The functional groups of CQDs can interact with the uncoordinated Pb2+ in the bottom surface of perovskite film to passivate defects and inhibit non-radiative recombination. The optimal concentration of CQDs added in SnO2 was investigated by measuring photovoltaic performance of the CQD-SnO2 PSCs with different CQDs concentrations, as shown in Fig. S8. The PSCs based on 1.4 wt% CQD-SnO2 have the highest photovoltaic performance. The current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the champion devices based on control and CQD-SnO2 ETL are shown in Fig. 5b. Compared with the control device, CQD-SnO2-based device has increased V oc from 1.13 V to 1.15 V, J sc from 23.64 to 25.62 mA cm−2, and FF from 80.71% to 81.22%. As a result, the champion PCE is increased from 21.62% to 24.05%. Fig. S9 shows the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra of the two PSCs. The integrated J sc values were 22.48 and 23.88 mA cm−2 for control and CQD-SnO2-based devices, respectively, consistent with the J sc determined by J-V curves. The steady-state photocurrent output and the maximum power point (MPP) tracking curves within 400 s are shown in Fig. S10. The stabilized PCEs for the control and CQD-SnO2-based device are 20.93% and 23.19% respectively, and almost no attenuation for MPP can be observed after 400s continuous tracking. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is commonly used to analyze the carrier transfer kinetics in PSCs [34]. By comparing the two Nyquist plots, the charge transfer resistance (R
t , reflected by the semicircle at high frequency) for the CQD-SnO2-based PSCs is smaller than that of the control device, while the impedance related to charge recombination (R
c ) reflected at low frequency is larger than that of the control device, as shown in Fig. S10. Detailed parameters were obtained by fitting the Nyquist plots using a widely applied equivalent circuit (the insert of Fig. S11), as shown in Table S2. The EIS results suggest the improved interfacial charge transfer and inhibited charge recombination. Apart from EIS, transient photovoltage (TPV) decay was also used to evaluate the degree of charge recombination [35]. As shown in Fig. S12, the raw data were fitted using a single exponential decay model to get the time constants of charge carrier recombination (τ R). Compared with control device (τ
R = 0.06 ms), the CQD-SnO2 device has a larger value (0.28 ms), suggesting a longer lifetime for free carriers owing to inhibited recombination. In order to better understand the effect of CQDs on the charge recombination process in the imbedded ETL/Perovskite interface, the J-V curves under different light intensities were measured. In the absence of trap-assisted recombination, the slope should be 1.0 kT/q. The deviation from the ideal value indicates that trap-assisted charge recombination takes place in all these devices [36]. It can be seen from Fig. S13 that the PSCs slopes of control and CQD-SnO2 are 1.33 kT/q and 1.11 kT/q respectively. The decreased slope of the PSCs based on CQD-SnO2 indicates that the trap-induced charge recombination has been effectively inhibited. We compared the dark I–V characteristics of control and CQD-SnO2-based PSCs (Fig. S14). The parameters of shunt resistance (R
sh ) and series resistance (R
s ) were calculated at open circuit and short circuit regions [37]. The CQD-SnO2 PSC shows a lower R
s (13.97 Ω cm2) and a larger R
sh (2.83 × 106 Ω cm2) than control PSCs (R
s = 18.76 Ω cm2，R
sh = 9.0 × 105 Ω cm2). Furthermore, the reverse saturated current density (J
0 ) has been calculated for the two PSCs. For the CQD-SnO2-based device has a J
0 of 2.66 × 10−10 mA cm−2, much lower than that of the control device (J
0 = 2.04 × 10−9 mA cm−2), which is favorable for promoting the photovoltaic performance.

Finally, we compared the long-term operational durability of the two devices by tracking their MPP in a nitrogen-filled glove box equipped with a white-LED light source (100 mW cm−2). As shown in Fig. 5c, the CQD-SnO2-based device retained over 82% of its initial power output after 1020 h continuous irradiation. By comparison, the maximum output of the control device declined notably, with only 52% of its initial output retained. Meanwhile, the cross-section SEM of PSCs after the light stability tests have been conducted, as shown in Fig. 5d and 5e. Obviously, the decomposed perovskite has appeared at the ETL/perovskite interface in the contrast sample. While a close connected ETL/perovskite interface was seen in the CQD-SnO2-based device, indicating that CQD-SnO2 ETL is more conducive to the long-term stability of SnO2/perovskite interface. In addition, the CQD-SnO2-based device also exhibited better thermal stability as it could retain 80% of its initial PCE after 500 h storage in dark at 85 °C, as shown in Fig. 5f. By comparison, only 65% of the initial PCE could be maintained by the control device under the same condition. The cross-section SEM of PSCs after thermal stability tests are shown in Fig. 5g and 5h. Severe structural decompose was observed at the perovskite/transport layers interfaces of the control device after stayed in oven at 85 °C for 500 h. The results validate that the CQD-SnO2-based PSCs are more stable than the control device. In addition to thermal stability and light stability, the shelf stability of PSCs was also tested. The shelf stability test was carried out by storing the devices in dark condition (under 30% relative humidity at room temperature). It can be seen from Fig. S15 that around 73% and 99% of the initial PCEs were retained by the control and CQD-SnO2-based devices, respectively. This improvement should be attributed to strengthened interfacial contact, reduced interfacial defects, and tuned the crystallization of perovskite with larger grains with fewer boundaries and defects on the CQDs buried in ETL.

In summary, the CQDs derived from CA and NR were embedded in SnO2 as ETL for improving the performance of PSCs. A holistic improvement on ETL and bottom contact between ETL and FAPbI3 perovskite was demonstrated by a variety of sophisticated characterizations, including enhance the electron mobility, reduce interface defects, guide perovskite crystallization into large grains, and enhance the charge transfer kinetics at interface between ETL and perovskite. The multifunctional groups distributed on the surface of CQDs can passivate interface defects, inhibit non-radiative recombination, and further improve the device stability. Moreover, the buried CQDs can improve the energy level alignment between ETL and perovskite layer. As a result of the above advantages, the PCE of PSCs with the imbedded CQDs in ETL is increased from 21.62% to 24.05%, and the stability is also improved significantly. This work provides a facile and efficient interface modification method for improving the performance and operational stability of FAPbI3-based PSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52272193, 12104081), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. DUT22GJ201), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC2007038, XLYC2008032), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2022T150087), and the Dalian Science and Technology Innovation Fund (No. 2022JJ12GX023).
